title: Rial Approaches Record Low as Economic Pressure Reaches Breaking Point
detail: The rial trades at approximately 1,430,000-1,440,000 per dollar on the eve of what will become Iran's largest protest movement since 1979. The currency has lost roughly 50% of its value in six months, from around 700,000 in January 2025 to 900,000 by mid-year and now approaching 1.44 million. Exchange shops in Tehran report being overwhelmed with sellers, with some refusing to transact altogether as the rate changes hourly. Black market activity has moved largely to encrypted messaging platforms.

media.caption: Rial approaches record low on the eve of Iran's largest protest movement since 1979

title: Tehran Grand Bazaar Merchants Coordinate Shutdown Plans
detail: Merchants across Tehran's Grand Bazaar circulate messages on social media and encrypted platforms coordinating a shutdown for the following day. Mobile phone sellers in the Alaeddin and Charsoo complexes report a 60% drop in sales as customers cannot afford goods that must be repriced daily. The bazaar, historically a barometer of Iranian social stability and a trigger for revolutionary movements, has not seen coordinated protest activity of this scale since the 1979 revolution. Guild leaders warn that a full bazaar strike is imminent.

media.caption: Tehran Grand Bazaar merchants coordinate unprecedented shutdown plans

title: Pezeshkian Admits Budget Cannot Match Wages to Inflation
detail: President Masoud Pezeshkian tells parliament the government 'cannot afford to raise wages in line with inflation,' a stark admission of fiscal incapacity that is broadcast on state media. The statement, coming days before the largest protests in the Islamic Republic's history, becomes a lightning rod for public anger. Opposition figures and labor unions seize on the admission as evidence that the regime has abandoned the social contract with its citizens.

media.caption: President Pezeshkian admits government cannot afford to match wages to inflation
